speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Kurt Saunders, August 16, 1996. Had you any idea then that you would-- what was going to happen or was-- were the concentration camps known at that time to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The concentration camps were known to me in Vienna, in Austria, because as soon as the Nazis walked in--

speaker: interviewer
text: They made them.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- first of all, they were sending Gentiles, Christians to concentration camp, Socialists, Communists, and others. They call it re-education.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Brainwashing. And many of the healthy, real healthy people that left for the concentration camp, they came back in an urn. And these were not Jews in the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were some Jews too, because you had Socialist Jews, Communist Jews. And they didn't re-educate them. They just got rid of him. But other Christians they knew that this is it. I mean, it was held over their heads. They knew that there was a way that they could punish them. So it was known. I mean, Dachau was in existence already. We didn't know anything--

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was all known to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you had no-- you did not know where they were.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew where Dachau was.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't know that they were elsewhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Were you able to-- were you ever reimbursed for that money?

speaker: interviewee
text: For what?

speaker: interviewer
text: For their tickets?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I got the money back.

speaker: interviewer
text: You got the money back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That became my engagement ring. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. This is we're talking already 1942. The war is on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The war in on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you when the war started? Were you in any way drafted or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not 'til 1943, yes. And my brother and I were drafted about the same time, meanwhile in--

speaker: interviewer
text: As what? Were-- you weren't Americans yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how were you drafted?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1941, after Pearl Harbor, my brother got married. They figured maybe if he got married, it would keep them out of the army for a while. He married Milie, cousin Milie. And he was married to her for 53 years, 'til he died. It was a real love affair. And I one day about two years later, 1940-- yeah, 1942, I came up to my brother's place, his apartment he was living in. And I walked in and I got into the living room and there is her girlfriend Florence sitting on the couch. As a matter of fact, they made a party for me a week before. And here she is on the couch. I didn't expect here to be there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I walked in. Milie says, do you remember my girlfriend, Flo? That was the abbreviation, Flo. I says, yes. I says, hi, shook her hand and I walked out. And I said, where's Walter? She says, in the kitchen fixing up drinks. Walked into the kitchen. I says, hi, Walter. He says, did you see Florence? I said, yes. I said, I'm going to marry her. And my brother says, you're nuts. How about your girlfriend Fredda? I said, did I tell you about my girlfriend Fredda? I said, I'm going to marry Florence. And that's what happened. I married Florence. Did that make you an American citizen then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. When I-- it happened this way. I was drafted, went into the Army. And I said, I'm not going to get married until I know that I'm halfway safe if I went up in another Pioneer Corps, you know, something like this in the American Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: As it happened, I had a very unique experience in the Army. When I was inducted into the Army, at Grand Central Palace in New York, they stamped my papers 'special assignment.' And I asked, what's a special assignment? Nobody would tell me. It's a secret. You'll find out soon enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: I arrived at Camp Atterbury, Indiana at the 83rd Infantry Division, the Austrian battalion. As I checked in, I got my duffel bag, my rifle, and everything else. That-- I knew Austrian battalion, there's such a thing as an Austrian battalion. Got to the barracks and then it really hit me. There were groups of people, each different places. One group spoke German. The other group spoke Yugoslav, Czech, Hungarian. The Austrian battalion was a people-- a group of people that at one time were ruled by the Austrian emperor before the First World War. And these were people that come out of that area. And each one was a little group. I said, I'm in the Foreign Legion. And then discovered the non-commissioned officers and the officers, their parents had come from that area.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I went back to the barracks the same-- within half an hour and I spoke to the first sergeant. I says, what am I doing here? This is not the America Army. This is the Foreign Legion.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, you're wearing an American uniform. The American flag is flying up there. It's an American Army. Naturally in the Army-- anybody knows the Army, your name becomes mud if you protest. And he gave me an order to report back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And right then and there, I was put in special detail. The first job was the hot belly stove had to be shined. And each night, it was 1:00, 2 o'clock in the morning, got in. Well, after a few nights, I knew I had to do something to get out of this outfit. I asked the first sergeant, can I write to the president of the United States? He says, why not? You write a letter. You give it to the corporal and it goes through the ranks.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to myself, my god, the war will be over. Little did I know how long the war's going to last. The war would be over before the president ever gets it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I wrote a different letter. I have a copy of that letter. If I could write to the President of the United States, this is what I have to say. This is supposed to be, I'm told, a voluntary outfit. I didn't volunteer for it. I have no interest. I'm not-- any desire to go back to Austria. I don't like to see the two Habsburgs, two brothers of Adolf Habsburg's were standing there with a coat straight over the shoulder everybody like Napoleon.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I don't want to go back to Austria. I'm an American now. I'm here. This is where I'm going to stay. And I sent a letter to Milie, my brother's wife. And I said, Milie, you send it to the president. And she did. And it didn't take long. It was less than a week. It went from the president to Major General Ulio in Omaha, Nebraska who was in charge of that whole area, and a special commission came down. Then they lined up the whole battalion for an interview and we all passed through it. Before we went through, the first sergeant and the lieutenant came over to warn us that if we say anything, that [INAUDIBLE], especially they were brainwashing me, that I'm going to have-- basic training, I didn't start yet. I'm going to be sent an outfit that's going to the Pacific to fight the Japanese. And you know what's going to happen to you. Well, I'm stubborn. When I went through, I told them the same thing. I don't care. I don't mind being in the American Army. As a matter of fact, I like to be. However, I don't want to be in the Austrian battalion. I don't have-- I don't care for Austria. Because what happened in Austria, I have written Austria off.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where--

speaker: interviewee
text: For me, Austria doesn't exist.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. So what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three or four days later, I'm told that I'm being shipped out. They didn't tell me where to or what about. But the first sergeant, I had to pack all my things in the two duffel bags that every soldier carries, put everything in there. And it was pouring rain. And as soon as we got outside the barracks, he said, inspection, open it up and put it down. I had to put all my things down in the mud. And then he said, now pack it again. Maybe I steal something. And now he says 'on the double.' That means 'run.' The train is going to leave. You're going to miss the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now I have been in the Army long enough-- it was my first month, the month wasn't over yet, so I used the F word. I had nothing to lose. What can I lose now? I am being shipped out, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used it. And I walked and I made it there. And I had the second surprise on the train. I was the only private. But there was a cadre, people that were pulled out from the 83rd Infantry Division to start a new division, the 75th Infantry Division at Fort Leonard Wood. And here I'm the only private on the train. I arrived in Fort Leonard Wood and division headquarters.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the morning, as we fall-- fall out, they call us out. They ask who can handle a typewriter, who can do this, who can do that? And naturally, we were told before, never volunteer for anything in the Army.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- but I found out that the softest job, the best job in the Army is a cook. So when they ask for a cook, I raise my arm. I says, I'm a cook. Well, I've been in the Army for three weeks already, 2 and 1/2 weeks. Doesn't take much to be a cook in the Army. You know, Buddy Hackett has the joke. He thought his mother was the best cook, right, in the whole world, 'til he joined the Army. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm a cook. So I was called in to the commanding officer's office. And he says, where did your cook? I says, in England, in Kitchener Camp. I warmed up some food at night. I was on guard duty there. But I always liked to cook. I'm a cook now. So I became the cook in the officers-- not-- in the soldiers' mess. There were two mess halls, three mess halls in division it was, one for the office, two for the enlisted men. I became the cook. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: It didn't take long. We had a mess sergeant that fouled up. I became the acting mess sergeant. And I was a corporal before the month was over, no basic training. 12 days later, I was a sergeant, promoted because the cook is supposed to be a sergeant. Now I became the acting mess sergeant. Needless to say, I had the best food. I had filet mignon for breakfast and everything else. And when we went out, the line companies would go out on-- in the field for three weeks out of four weeks. Division headquarters only went out one of four weeks. And then your ride on the truck. You don't walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And how long did all of this last?

speaker: interviewee
text: This lasted 'til the company was supposed to go-- by the way, I took an IQ test in the Army when you joined. And I was in the group-- I had 138 IQ test. And I qualified for going to college. But I didn't want to. Meanwhile, I had gotten married during the war. And I'd brought my wife out to the camp. And she was staying in the Methodist chaplain's house. And I had a wonderful setup here.

speaker: interviewer
text: But anyway, to come to-- back to this. The company is going on maneuvers to Louisiana. What the heck am I going to do in Louisiana with the chiggers and eaten up with the mosquitoes and everything else? Now I decided I want to go to college.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went up to headquarters. I had made a lot of friends. They knew about it. They also knew that finally, the Austrian battalion was busted up and the first sergeant became the first sergeant of my company. And he told them in the office, I'm going to be the first man that's going to bust him, take the stripes away. When I heard this, I told my friends up in the headquarters and they shipped the first sergeant out onto the line.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now I would like to go to college. So I go up there and they said, well, didn't you read the papers? Congress voted down. No more college for soldiers. There's the last train. It's going out next Wednesday-- and this was a week ahead-- to college. And that's the end of the whole ASTP, Army Specialized Training Program. I said, are the officers still around that passed me the first time? He says, yes. I said, where they are? So he gave me the names. I went to the four officers, took my papers. They signed for me. They sent it out again to Major General Ulio in Omaha, Nebraska. And I was on the train. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Florence went back to New York. And I'm shipped out now. I wind up in Grinnell, Iowa, Grinnell College for the next 12 weeks. There were 145 of us to start.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did-- what were you being trained for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I took-- there were four subjects, geopolitics, German-- what was the other one? I got the diploma home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this--

speaker: interviewee
text: History.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this training that would lead to something in the Army, military?

speaker: interviewee
text: Military government.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did that affect what you were going to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, none of us knew what's going to happen, because at the end of the 12 weeks, I was called into headquarters. And out of the 145 that started, there were seven left, and I was one of the seven.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. And what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: And of the seven, six had college diplomas, master degrees. Some were from Oxford University. One was out of France. Another one was out of England.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was the only one that didn't have a degree, right? But I was part of that group, because my marks were high enough. My average was 97. What was the goal of the group? What was the purpose?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't tell us. They don't tell you anything in the Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't tell you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, they called me at headquarters. They said, you'll be overseas before you know. Meanwhile, Flow was there too. She had come out to Iowa. 2 o'clock in the morning, they stopped the train. We-- send us to Chicago, from Chicago to New York. I had five days. And I wound up in Boston and was shipped overseas and I arrived in Manchester, England. That's when we found out we're going to be military government. Now we started to study the town that we're going to govern.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what town was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mainz, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mainz. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: So we started ahead of time. But we knew who to see. The bishop was against the Nazis. We knew the people we should get in touch-- if they're still alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What war-- what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1943. Yeah. The fall-- the winter of '43, '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: The war was still on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: War ended in '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Mainz-- no, the war ended in '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mainz was in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were going to be a military government in Mainz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of Mainz.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the war was over or won? The assumption--

speaker: interviewee
text: As soon as they occupy it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. So what-- did you see action? What did you do until you could occupy it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- I landed. I was sent in on D-Day plus 3 into France and Omaha Beach. We got to the beach. We had high priority. But if you go back in history, you realize we did not advance as fast as expected, so we sat on the barge, a landing barge, for another four days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK? Then we landed. We advanced into France. On the way into France, we had-- we're in France. We are not called military government. We are called civil affairs, 'affaires civiles' in French. We had one town that we governed for a while. Then we wound up in Bastogne, after the Germans broke through in Bastogne. We were moved up with Patton's Third Army. And we took a town-- we governed the town Houffalize before Bastogne. The we got Bastogne. And after that, we moved on again. Each time--

speaker: interviewer
text: In this time, did you get to any camps at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There were no camps yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were no camps yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were still in France.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from France, we went into Luxembourg and Liechtenstein. Then we went into Germany. And there was a retreat. We-- Herr Patton had advanced into Germany, into Strasbourg. And he was forced to pull back to [FRENCH], France. Also, all along I was being an interpreter. I was loaned out to different groups. For example, there was an investigation going on by the criminal investigation division, and we were sent out trying to trace the happened-- what happened to some of America equipment. Then I was asked-- I was sent out again in a Jeep when Metz fell. In Metz, the Germans blew up the bridges before the Gestapo could escape, and we caught the Gestapo. So our team, there were four of us, were sent in there to question the Gestapo.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these were some of the jobs I had in between. Sometimes I was gone for a week, for 10 days, sometimes two weeks. And then I came back to my outfit again. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And finally we got in-- after Bastogne and all the things, different things happened, finally we got into Germany. And we've made it to Mainz. And in Mainz, we became the military government of Mainz. We took over whole area, one of the suburbs. Mainz was bombed for 22 minutes and completely destroyed, completely. But the suburbs of Mainz were still all right. And we cleaned out the whole area of Mainz. I had a commanding officer who was of German background. As a matter of fact, I experienced some anti-Semitism in American Army too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Originally when I was a mess sergeant, there was a mess sergeant in another mess hall, the other-- there were two mess halls, [INAUDIBLE]. And I had a habit that I never followed the menu that was given to us. I-- special, as a matter of fact, mess halls were rated-- and again, not to brag, but I'm bragging about it. My mess hall was the number one in the whole division. They finally issued tickets, because everybody was coming to the mess hall, eating in our place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this mess sergeant came in. He was upset because I didn't follow the menu and he did. And we got the reputation having such a wonderful mess hall. So he started to get ornery. And then one [INAUDIBLE] said to another one, he says, if you didn't have those stripes, he says, you Jew bastard, he says, I'll beat the heck out of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: For a minute I felt like to take my jacket off. And I said to myself, it's going to cost me the stripes if I have a fight with him. I've worked hard enough to become a sergeant.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took him to the commanding officer, who was Pennsylvania Dutch-- his name was Fluch, F-L-U-C-H-- and I filed a complaint. And our commanding officer said, you know, Sergeant, he says, you're supposed to take orders. He's superior, outranks you. But he wasn't entitled to do what he did. And I agreed with the officer. And the end of the story is they took this guy-- he was an alcoholic on top of it-- they shipped them out. So-- And while you were in this area, did you have any contact with Jews who were coming out of camps or Jews who were coming out of hiding in the Mainz area?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we march into Mainz, the sergeant that's marching with the infantry division, the group of Germans that greeted him, the first person to greet him was his own father, who had been in hiding. He was a college professor and he'd been in hiding in Mainz. Yes, I had met some Jews that had been in hiding. Unique thing was that some of those Jews, particularly one that became the police chief-- he was not Jewish but his wife was, and his son-- were hidden by big-shot Nazis whose family was all involved with the Gestapo, with the Air Force, and everything else. And when I asked them, how come, did you know that you were losing the war, he said, when I joined the Nazi Party before Hitler came to power, he said, I felt that he was the messiah. I found out soon enough he was not the messiah. And when the orders came that Mainz has to be judenrein, that all the Jews have to be deported-- the expression was, when you get all the Jews out, judenrein, clean of Jews-- that all the Jews will be deported, so I-- I helped these people because I felt [GERMAN]. In those days actually, we were winning the war, in '43, he says, but I felt that nobody would suspect me that I have hidden Jews, because with my daughter in the Air Force, my son with the Gestapo, and they're all involved with the Nazis and I'm a Golden Party member-- I joined before Hitler came to power-- nobody would ever suspect me to hide Jews. He says, that's what I did. And he did. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: What other experiences did you have with Jews who were coming out of hiding or out of camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, these were about the only people. Later, when the war was over, I also discovered that my father's sister, her husband, and two of her children, the one that was in camp with me, died. But her daughter and her son were hiding in Berlin. There were 117 Jews that were hiding in Berlin through the war. She survived by hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: But this was not somebody that you had--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. The only other people I met is the ones we brought back from Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us a little bit about how that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were-- they let us know that there's Jewish survivors in Theresienstadt that used to live in Mainz.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- was Theresienstadt liberated at that point? Did they let you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yes. They needed transportation to take them home.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went there, brought them back.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see when you got to Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Regular camp, nothing-- didn't look any worse than any Army camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what did it look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Barracks. And some-- some people waiting. There were not too many people left there. Some people waiting to go home or be taken home. Lots of people had to return to the different places that they came from.

speaker: interviewer
text: When it had been liberated? How long before you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Couple of weeks, that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's all.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had one other incident, too, in Mainz. We had-- we had a camp, DP camp, displaced person camp, across the river, the other side of the Mainz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which camp was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember the name. And there were about 16,000 displaced persons in there. They were not Jews. They were Gentiles. There were maybe some Jews too. They were people that had been brought into Germany by the Nazis to work there, forced labor. And when the war was over, all the different nationalities, the French and the Belgium, all these different nationalities had been in Germany. They tried to return home and we stopped them because we needed the roads open, the highways open. The only people that didn't want to return were the Russians. They did want to go back. It was an agreement made in the autumn between Stalin and President Roosevelt and Churchill that all the Russian displaced persons will go back to Russia. And the Russian team came in. And they were sitting in the office to talk to my commanding officer and I was out there, talked to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there's this little Russian officers there with the high cheekbones, blond hair, blue eyes. And all of a sudden he turns around to me and he says to me [RUSSIAN]? I said, yes, why do you ask? He says, I am Jewish. But they don't know, pointing at those Russian soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, I come from the Ukraine. He says, all my people, my family, were killed by the Nazis. I escaped, he says, and I joined the underground. But if they had know that I I'm a Jew, they would have killed me too, send me right back to the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he says, they don't know it. So this was a guy, the team that came in to take the Russians back to Russia. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we put them on the trains. They jumped off the trains. They didn't want to go back. I don't know whether you're aware of they sealed the trains. Did you know that?

speaker: interviewer
text: The what?

speaker: interviewee
text: They sealed the trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. No, I wasn't. But these were not Jews. These were--

speaker: interviewee
text: These were displaced persons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Displaced persons who were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly Christians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the American Army sealed the trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. I did not know that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Didn't want to go home.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to get back out to Theresienstadt. I'd like to get-- like to know how many people were inmates of Theresienstadt were from Mainz and how many did you find?

speaker: interviewer
text: 14. They were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The only survivors.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were they the only ones there--

speaker: interviewee
text: Only survivors.

speaker: interviewer
text: --when you got there? When you got to Theresienstadt, were there other Jews there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we didn't look at anybody. These are the people waiting for us. We took them and we went.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: These people didn't want to stay there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much time did you spend in Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe an hour. When this-- you realize when you were there, you-- you don't want to see. You don't want to see the pain. You don't want to see this. Today, these places, Dachau, Auschwitz, have become tourists places. In those days, it-- it was-- it's like if you-- if you're sick and you get well, you want to get out of the hospital in a hurry. You don't want to stay in the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: These people know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the people you were with know you were Jewish, your American soldiers knew you were Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. Never hid it. As a matter of fact, we had the first Passover service in Paris. And it was conducted by Jews, but helped by a Catholic chaplain. He got us all the stuff to have a Passover service.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did these people tell you when they saw you? What-- what was their reaction when they saw you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were happy, but they didn't talk much. They didn't talk much because these were not really-- these people had a position there. They were in the warehouse that took the stuff off the prisoners and then put them on the shelf. The jewelry went here and this was there. They had a privileged position. They were fed better too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I don't know whether they had a guilt complex. There was never too much talk about it. The only thing is that when they got back to my Mainz, they started to demand a lot of things. How did they get back-- how did you get them back?

speaker: interviewee
text: We brought them back by truck, American Army truck.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did they take with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: What they have on their backs, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they, men, women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Both.

speaker: interviewer
text: Both.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you converse in?

speaker: interviewee
text: German.

speaker: interviewer
text: In German. What happened when they got back to Mainz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we had nothing to do with them after this. This was taken over. They just disappeared within the town. I guess they had neighbors they must have known from before and so on. We-- they got-- it was a different department of our outfit. We gave them apartments that had been confiscated from the Germans, you know. And originally when-- the first time when we moved out Germans out of the area that we occupied, there was one family was this fellow who had survived with his wife and son. And I came out and told my commanding officer, he is a survivor. He said, the Germans are in there. Tell them to get out. So I told them to get out.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that some of these people were not very nice that you brought in.

speaker: interviewee
text: No they were not.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were not. Their general behavior. But when you started thinking-- later on you start to think about it, you say to yourself, would I have been any different? But at first, it's a shock, because what they have been through, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you're not used to the kind of behavior.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to change the tape now and I want to go a little bit more into what kind of behavior you're talking about.
